headings.text: Dead Water: The Slow Poisoning of the Ogallala Aquifer
The Disappearing Well: Texas's 117,000 Orphaned Boreholes
Corn, Cattle, and the Math of Subsidized Drought
The Nitrate Belt: A Rural Cancer Map Nobody Drew

paragraphs.text: The pump at the edge of Section 14 has been running for fifty-one years, drawing from a body of water older than the Pleistocene. On the morning we arrive, it whines like a tired animal — a thin, metallic complaint that the farmer who owns it, Russell Doerr, says began sometime last August. The well used to draw clean from 84 feet. It now reaches 217 feet, and the water that surfaces smells faintly of iron and something else, something organic and wrong.

This is what aquifer collapse sounds like in 2024: not a roar, but a wheeze. The Ogallala — the great underground sea that stretches beneath eight states from South Dakota to Texas — is being drained six times faster than it can recharge. In some counties of the Texas Panhandle, hydrologists no longer speak in years; they speak in months. The water that remains is increasingly contaminated by the very industries that depleted the volume above it: feedlot nitrate plumes, petrochemical chloride bleed from abandoned wells, and a rising tide of arsenic concentration as the saturated thickness contracts.

Over eighteen months, Fracture Line Dispatch reviewed 4,200 pages of state-level water quality reports, 1,100 EPA enforcement letters obtained through FOIA, and conducted on-the-ground sampling in 47 wells across five states. What emerged is a portrait of a slow-motion disaster the federal government has refused to formally name.

The Ogallala does not deplete uniformly. It thins. Picture a slab of saturated gravel — in places once 1,200 feet thick — losing two feet of vertical water column each year. As the saturated thickness drops below 30 feet, well yields collapse exponentially. Below 15 feet, pumps cavitate. Below 6 feet, the well is functionally dead. In Hartley County, Texas, 38% of registered agricultural wells crossed that final threshold between 2018 and 2023.

What complicates the arithmetic of loss is what hydrologists call the concentration effect. As volume shrinks, dissolved solids — nitrates from a half-century of fertilizer percolation, sulfates from feedlot runoff, naturally occurring arsenic and uranium leaching from contact with newly exposed mineral surfaces — concentrate in the remaining water. A well that tested at 4 mg/L of nitrate in 2010 may now test at 14 mg/L without any new contamination event. The poison did not increase. The water decreased.

In Stevens County, Kansas, the rural water district has installed three reverse-osmosis treatment systems in five years. The cost, borne by 1,840 residents, is $7.2 million and rising. The cost, borne by the corporate agricultural operators whose wells extract 87% of the county's groundwater, is zero.

The 1986 Safe Drinking Water Act amendments classified the Ogallala as a “sole source aquifer” — a federal designation theoretically triggering enhanced EPA review of any project that might contaminate it. In the 38 years since, the EPA has formally invoked that authority exactly four times. Three of those invocations were withdrawn after industry challenge. The fourth, in 2003, resulted in a $14,000 settlement.

State-level regulators are no more aggressive. Texas's Groundwater Conservation Districts, the primary regulatory bodies, are governed by boards on which sitting agricultural operators routinely hold majorities. In our review of 31 districts, 22 had never issued a permit denial. Seventeen had never imposed a fine. Four did not employ a full-time hydrologist.

Inside the affected counties, the political vocabulary for the crisis remains narrow. Local newspapers describe “challenges.” Extension agents speak of “transitions.” Even Doerr, whose well now reaches more than twice as deep as it once did, hesitates before saying the word collapse. “You don't say it,” he tells us, leaning on the pump housing. “If you say it, the land's already gone.”

The most damning finding of our investigation may also be the most quiet: in 14 of the 23 counties where saturated thickness has crossed below the 30-foot operational threshold, no state agency has issued a public advisory. Residents continue to drink, irrigate, and bathe with water whose contaminant load has roughly tripled since 2015. They are not being told. They are not, in the bureaucratic sense, being lied to. They are simply not being addressed.

The story of the Ogallala has, for forty years, been told as a story of slow loss. Our reporting suggests this framing is no longer adequate. The aquifer is not slowly disappearing. It is being actively transformed into something else — a smaller, more concentrated, more toxic remnant whose contamination will outlast the depletion event by centuries. The water table that returns, if it returns, will return into a poisoned vessel.

Russell Doerr's well will not last another decade. He knows this. His neighbors know this. What none of them know — what no one has told them — is whether the water they pull from the ground today is already past the threshold at which it should be drunk.

Selected counties drawn from the eight-state Ogallala overlay. Aquifer depth measured to the top of the saturated zone. Decline rate annualized over the full interval. Risk tier reflects projected operational viability through 2035.
An accounting of the abandoned oil and gas wells the state has lost track of — and the contamination pathways they create into the High Plains Aquifer.
How federal crop insurance restructured the economics of irrigation across the Plains, creating financial incentives to drain the aquifer faster than it can recharge.
A two-year correlation study of agricultural nitrate exposure and pediatric blue-baby syndrome incidence across nine Kansas counties.

One investigation, one data brief, one field note. Sent every Thursday morning.

visibleText: Fracture Line . Dispatch Independent Investigative Journalism Vol. VI · No. 142 · Investigation Front Investigation Data Desk Field Notes About Investigation · Water & Land · 18-Month Series Dead Water: The Slow Poisoning of the Ogallala Aquifer By Marin Velázquez & Theo Halliday March 14, 2024 22 min read Filed from Garden City, KS Topsoil & Loess · 0–8 m Caprock Clay · 8–24 m Ogallala Sand & Gravel · 24–110 m Permian Bedrock — 1.9 ft / yr drawdown Irrigation Wells The pump at the edge of Section 14 has been running for fifty-one years, drawing from a body of water older than the Pleistocene. On the morning we arrive, it whines like a tired animal — a thin, metallic complaint that the farmer who owns it, Russell Doerr, says began sometime last August. The well used to draw clean from 84 feet. It now reaches 217 feet, and the water that surfaces smells faintly of iron and something else, something organic and wrong. This is what aquifer collapse sounds like in 2024: not a roar, but a wheeze. The Ogallala — the great underground sea that stretches beneath eight states from South Dakota to Texas — is being drained six times faster than it can recharge. In some counties of the Texas Panhandle, hydrologists no longer speak in years; they speak in months. The water that remains is increasingly contaminated by the very industries that depleted the volume above it: feedlot nitrate plumes, petrochemical chloride bleed from abandoned wells, and a rising tide of arsenic concentration as the saturated thickness contracts. Over eighteen months, Fracture Line Dispatch reviewed 4,200 pages of state-level water quality reports, 1,100 EPA enforcement letters obtained through FOIA, and conducted on-the-ground sampling in 47 wells across five states. What emerged is a portrait of a slow-motion disaster the federal government has refused to formally name. “We stopped using the word ‘sustainable’ in our internal memos in 2019. It became dishonest. There is no sustainable version of what is happening down there.” — Former USGS Regional Hydrologist, Lubbock Field Office The Geometry of Drawdown The Ogallala does not deplete uniformly. It thins. Picture a slab of saturated gravel — in places once 1,200 feet thick — losing two feet of vertical water column each year. As the saturated thickness drops below 30 feet, well yields collapse exponentially. Below 15 feet, pumps cavitate. Below 6 feet, the well is functionally dead. In Hartley County, Texas, 38% of registered agricultural wells crossed that final threshold between 2018 and 2023. CROSS-SECTION · BUFFALO LAKE Figure 1 A schematic cross-section of the Ogallala beneath Buffalo Lake National Wildlife Refuge, illustrating saturated-thickness contraction observed between 1998 and 2023. Vertical scale exaggerated 18×. Source: Fracture Line Dispatch field surveys; USGS NWIS data, Q3 2023. What complicates the arithmetic of loss is what hydrologists call the concentration effect . As volume shrinks, dissolved solids — nitrates from a half-century of fertilizer percolation, sulfates from feedlot runoff, naturally occurring arsenic and uranium leaching from contact with newly exposed mineral surfaces — concentrate in the remaining water. A well that tested at 4 mg/L of nitrate in 2010 may now test at 14 mg/L without any new contamination event. The poison did not increase. The water decreased. In Stevens County, Kansas, the rural water district has installed three reverse-osmosis treatment systems in five years. The cost, borne by 1,840 residents, is $7.2 million and rising. The cost, borne by the corporate agricultural operators whose wells extract 87% of the county's groundwater, is zero. “The aquifer is being privatized in real time. The water leaves as a commodity — corn, beef, ethanol. What stays behind is the bill.” — Lydia Renteria, Kansas Rural Water Council The Regulatory Silence The 1986 Safe Drinking Water Act amendments classified the Ogallala as a “sole source aquifer” — a federal designation theoretically triggering enhanced EPA review of any project that might contaminate it. In the 38 years since, the EPA has formally invoked that authority exactly four times. Three of those invocations were withdrawn after industry challenge. The fourth, in 2003, resulted in a $14,000 settlement. State-level regulators are no more aggressive. Texas's Groundwater Conservation Districts, the primary regulatory bodies, are governed by boards on which sitting agricultural operators routinely hold majorities. In our review of 31 districts, 22 had never issued a permit denial. Seventeen had never imposed a fine. Four did not employ a full-time hydrologist. FIELD SAMPLING · OCHILTREE COUNTY Figure 2 Reporter Marin Velázquez collects a 500ml grab sample from an abandoned irrigation well in Ochiltree County, Texas. The well was decommissioned in 2021 but never sealed, providing a direct conduit for surface contamination into the upper aquifer strata. Inside the affected counties, the political vocabulary for the crisis remains narrow. Local newspapers describe “challenges.” Extension agents speak of “transitions.” Even Doerr, whose well now reaches more than twice as deep as it once did, hesitates before saying the word collapse. “You don't say it,” he tells us, leaning on the pump housing. “If you say it, the land's already gone.” “What we are documenting is not depletion. Depletion implies a finite process toward zero. This is a phase change — the conversion of a renewable resource into a permanently degraded one.” — Dr. Anand Brewer, Texas Tech Hydrogeology Program The most damning finding of our investigation may also be the most quiet: in 14 of the 23 counties where saturated thickness has crossed below the 30-foot operational threshold, no state agency has issued a public advisory. Residents continue to drink, irrigate, and bathe with water whose contaminant load has roughly tripled since 2015. They are not being told. They are not, in the bureaucratic sense, being lied to. They are simply not being addressed. What Remains Beneath The story of the Ogallala has, for forty years, been told as a story of slow loss. Our reporting suggests this framing is no longer adequate. The aquifer is not slowly disappearing. It is being actively transformed into something else — a smaller, more concentrated, more toxic remnant whose contamination will outlast the depletion event by centuries. The water table that returns, if it returns, will return into a poisoned vessel. Russell Doerr's well will not last another decade. He knows this. His neighbors know this. What none of them know — what no one has told them — is whether the water they pull from the ground today is already past the threshold at which it should be drunk. EPA FOIA Release 24-EPA-0431 1,143 pages of enforcement correspondence concerning the Ogallala Sole Source Aquifer designation, 1986–2023. Key Documents EPA Memo 2019-WT-114: Internal review of SSA enforcement gaps USGS Open-File Report 2022-1086: Saturated thickness modeling, 1995–2020 Kansas GMD-3 Board Minutes, March 2021 (redacted release) Ogallala Aquifer Initiative Final Programmatic Report, USDA-ARS Texas Tech HGP Working Paper 23-04: Concentration effects under drawdown Fracture Line Dispatch field log, Aug 2022 – Feb 2024 Reporter's Note The phrase “sole source aquifer” appears in fewer than 40% of state-level public advisories issued since 2015 in counties overlying the Ogallala. Where it does appear, it is most often in materials prepared by environmental NGOs, not regulators. We asked four state agencies why. None responded on the record. Decline rate annualized over the full interval. Risk tier reflects projected operational viability through 2035.
